response: [The indigenous community member is an expert in tracking elephant herds.] [They have spent many years observing the movements of elephants in the reserve.] [Today, they noticed an unusual elephants.] [This herd is known for its calm behavior.] [The elephants in this herd are often seen near the riverbanks.] [The second herd is twice as large as the first herd.] [This means the second herd has double the number of elephants in the first herd.] [Twice 12 is 24.] [So the second herd has 24 elephants.] [This herd is more active and often moves quickly through the forest.] [The third herd is 5 elephants smaller than the second herd.] [This means we need to subtract 5 from the number of elephants in the second herd.] [24 minus 5 is 19.] [So the third herd has 19 elephants.] [This herd is known for its strong family bonds.] [Each herd has its own unique characteristics.] [The first herd is the smallest of the three.] [The second herd is the largest of the three.] [The third herd is in between in size.] [The total number of elephants is the sum of all three herds.] [Adding the numbers together will give the total.] [12 plus 24 is 36.] [36 plus 19 is 55.] [Therefore, there are 55 elephants at the waterhole.] [This is a significant number for a single waterhole.] [The gathering of these herds suggests a change in the environment.] [The elephants may have been driven together by a lack of water elsewhere.] [The indigenous member is watching closely to understand the situation.] [They are noting the behavior of each herd.] [The elephants are drinking, resting, and socializing.] [This is an important moment for the community.] [The member is sharing their observations with others.] [The information helps in planning for the future.] [The member is also ensuring
